creativeNotes: This is a strong pilot for the New School world — it earns its emotional beats instead of manufacturing them, gives every named character (Jordan, Mia, Alex) an internal logic that survives across branches, and structures its choice architecture so that 'confidence,' 'empathy,' and 'adaptability' are genuinely different playstyles rather than a good/bad binary. The lunchroom bridge-building beat, Mia's flicker of guilt, and Alex's small trust gestures (sliding the sketchbook, the dry robot joke) are all specific and well-observed rather than generic 'new kid' tropes. The branchDialogue callbacks in the ending scenes are a real craft strength — they make the player's morning choices feel remembered rather than forgotten once the plot moves on. There's a minor data artifact in branchDialogue (path-d-cost under scene-11a has a malformed entry with `"id": "player", "id2": null`), which should be cleaned up before production, but it's a data-hygiene issue, not a creative one.

story: The three-tier lunchroom convergence (Alex/Mia/Bridge) is well-paced and gives real weight to choice-3's group project moment by having it echo at lunch — good structural rhyme.

Consider one more beat of texture in scene-6 showing Ms. Patel's reaction (or lack thereof) to Mia's dig at Alex — right now the adult world is entirely absent from the crisis moment, which is realistic but a single line acknowledging that absence (a kid noticing no teacher stepped in) could sharpen the theme of social self-navigation.
Fix the malformed dialogue entry in branchDialogue for scene-11a/path-d-cost (id field collision) — pipeline hygiene, not a story issue.
character: Alex is the standout — 'It's dumb, forget it' as a recurring verbal tic before he's rewarded with attention is a genuinely observed piece of characterization for a kid used to being overlooked.
Mia avoids being a flat 'mean girl': her quiet 'don't tell anyone, but today was actually a good day' and the guilty glance across the cafeteria give her an interior life that pays off thematically.

Jordan's dual role as tour guide and low-key moral compass works, though watch that he doesn't become too wise/therapist-like in the reflection scenes — a couple of his lines in scene-10/11 verge on adult-authored wisdom rather than an eighth grader's voice (e.g., 'Doesn't mean it doesn't matter which one it is' reads slightly too polished).
dialogue: Player's internal voice ('Cool, cool, love it here already') is a nice consistent verbal tic that reads authentically Gen Z-adjacent without being try-hard slang.

A few Jordan lines in the resolution scenes could be trimmed or roughened up syntactically to sound less like thesis statements and more like a 13-year-old thinking out loud — e.g., 'That's not just adapting. That's, like, adapting with a purpose.' is close to being an essay thesis in dialogue form.
tone: Tone is consistent throughout — wry, warm, never mawkish. The choice to let 'siding with Mia' not be narratively punished (per the educational goals) is respected in the writing; scene-7c ends on discomfort rather than an artificial consequence, which is the right call.
Cafeteria-as-country framing in scene-8's narration is a nice small worldbuilding touch consistent with the 'social map' framing used throughout.

inspirationReferences.title: PEN15
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Balances comedic, self-aware internal narration with genuinely earned emotional vulnerability in middle-school social dynamics.

inspirationReferences.title: Wonder by R.J. Palacio
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Similar interest in how small, quiet social choices (who sits where, who speaks up) compound into a defining reputation.

inspirationReferences.title: Life is Strange
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Branching dialogue that remembers earlier choices and reflects them back later, as this episode does in its ending branchDialogue callbacks.
